[aviator](/wiki/Aircraftman "Aircraftman") | |

([UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): [/lɛfˈtɛnənt ˈkɜːrnəl/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*lef-TEN-ənt KUR-nəl*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), [US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/luːˈtɛn-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*loo-TEN-*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a rank of [commissioned officers](/wiki/Commissioned_officer "Commissioned officer") in the [armies](/wiki/Army "Army"), most [marine forces](/wiki/Marine_(armed_services) "Marine (armed services)") and some air forces of the world, above a [major](/wiki/Major_(military_rank) "Major (military rank)") and below a [colonel](/wiki/Colonel "Colonel"). Several police forces in the United States use the rank of [lieutenant](/wiki/Lieutenant "Lieutenant") [colonel](/wiki/Colonel "Colonel"). The rank of lieutenant colonel is often shortened to simply "colonel" in conversation and in unofficial correspondence. Sometimes, the term 'half-colonel' is used in casual conversation in the British Army. Additionally, in the U.S. Army 'light colonel' has been used informally in the past.[[1]](#cite_note-1) In the British military, it is customary to refer to either a Lieutenant Colonel or a Colonel by their first names when mentioning them, e.g. "Colonel Tim will be at the parade". In the United States Air Force, the term 'light bird' or 'light bird colonel' (as opposed to a 'full bird colonel') is an acceptable casual reference to the rank but is never used directly towards the rank holder.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] A lieutenant colonel is typically in charge of a [battalion](/wiki/Battalion "Battalion") or [regiment](/wiki/Regiment "Regiment") in the army.

**Lieutenant colonel**  ([UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): [/lɛfˈtɛnənt ˈkɜːrnəl/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*lef-TEN-ənt KUR-nəl*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), [US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/luːˈtɛn-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*loo-TEN-*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a rank of [commissioned officers](/wiki/Commissioned_officer "Commissioned officer") in the [armies](/wiki/Army "Army"), most [marine forces](/wiki/Marine_(armed_services) "Marine (armed services)") and some air forces of the world, above a [major](/wiki/Major_(military_rank) "Major (military rank)") and below a [colonel](/wiki/Colonel "Colonel"). Several police forces in the United States use the rank of [lieutenant](/wiki/Lieutenant "Lieutenant") [colonel](/wiki/Colonel "Colonel"). The rank of lieutenant colonel is often shortened to simply "colonel" in conversation and in unofficial correspondence. Sometimes, the term 'half-colonel' is used in casual conversation in the British Army. Additionally, in the U.S. Army 'light colonel' has been used informally in the past.[[1]](#cite_note-1) In the British military, it is customary to refer to either a Lieutenant Colonel or a Colonel by their first names when mentioning them, e.g. "Colonel Tim will be at the parade". In the United States Air Force, the term 'light bird' or 'light bird colonel' (as opposed to a 'full bird colonel') is an acceptable casual reference to the rank but is never used directly towards the rank holder.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] A lieutenant colonel is typically in charge of a [battalion](/wiki/Battalion "Battalion") or [regiment](/wiki/Regiment "Regiment") in the army.

The following articles deal with the rank of lieutenant colonel:

* [Lieutenant-colonel (Canada)](/wiki/Lieutenant-colonel_(Canada) "Lieutenant-colonel (Canada)")
* [Lieutenant colonel (Eastern Europe)](/wiki/Lieutenant_colonel_(Eastern_Europe) "Lieutenant colonel (Eastern Europe)")
* [Lieutenant colonel (Turkey)](/wiki/Lieutenant_colonel_(Turkey) "Lieutenant colonel (Turkey)")
* [Lieutenant colonel (Sri Lanka)](/wiki/Lieutenant_colonel_(Sri_Lanka) "Lieutenant colonel (Sri Lanka)")
* [Lieutenant colonel (United Kingdom)](/wiki/Lieutenant_colonel_(United_Kingdom) "Lieutenant colonel (United Kingdom)")[[2]](#cite_note-2)
* [Lieutenant colonel (United States)](/wiki/Lieutenant_colonel_(United_States) "Lieutenant colonel (United States)")
